meta.title: L663 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Land Rover Defender L663 engine — 204DT, AJ20D6, PT306, 5.0 V8. Real UK costs, Ingenium reliability data, and honest failure analysis. Get free quotes from 12+ vetted Land Rover specialists today.

@graph.name: Land Rover Defender L663 Engine Codes
Land Rover Defender L663 Core Variants

mainEntity.name: How much does a Land Rover Defender L663 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned L663 engine typically costs £3,000–£12,000 supply only, depending on engine — the 2.0-litre diesel sits at £3,500–£5,800, while the 5.0 V8 can reach £6,000–£12,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,430. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Land Rover Defender L663 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The AJ20D6 3.0-litre six-cylinder diesel (D250/D300/D350) is generally considered the most dependable L663 engine — it is more refined than the four-cylinder, with early reliability surveys suggesting fewer major failures. The 3.0 P400 petrol is also a strong contender.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Land Rover Defender L663?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain tensioner wear on the 2.0-litre diesel (204DT), turbocharger and emissions system failures on the 3.0-litre diesel (AJ20D6), and BISG belt/cooling fan issues on the 3.0-litre petrol (PT306) — plus the usual JLR electrical gremlins that affect all modern JLR products.

mainEntity.name: Is the Land Rover Defender L663 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Reliability is mixed. The 3.0-litre Ingenium engines — both diesel and petrol — have shown fewer major issues than the 2.0-litre four-cylinder diesel, which has a documented timing chain tensioner risk. Early L663 units (2019–2020) are more problematic than later production.

mainEntity.name: Which Land Rover Defender L663 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but a 2.0-litre diesel (D200/D240) without a documented service history and chain inspection is the one purchase we consistently advise against. A high-mileage 5.0 V8 without timing chain history is the second.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my Defender L663 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Cold-start rattle (2.0 diesel timing chain), whistling under acceleration or reduced power (3.0 diesel turbo), or overheating/check engine light (3.0 petrol cooling system) are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Land Rover Defender L663 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a £40,000 L663, a £5,000 AJ20D6 recon is 12.5% of the car's value — safe. Against a low-value high-mileage example, the same recon may approach 20–30% — still well within our 60% Land Rover threshold. On an L663, replacement almost always makes sense.

mainEntity.name: How long do Land Rover Defender L663 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 3.0-litre Ingenium diesels can exceed 150,000–200,000 miles with proper servicing. The 2.0-litre diesel's limiting factor is the timing chain — addressed proactively, it can also reach high mileage. The 5.0 V8 is similarly durable but requires specialist maintenance for the supercharger.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the Defender 90, 110 and 130?

acceptedAnswer.text: All three share the L663 platform and most engines. The Defender 90 is the 3-door short-wheelbase (2019–present), the 110 is the 5-door standard-wheelbase (2019–present), and the 130 is the extended 5-door (2022–present). Costs vary slightly by body style due to labour access — see each generation's own page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Land Rover Defender L663 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible on the engine itself. Tell us your variant (D200, P400, etc.) and approximate registration date and we'll identify it — 12+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Land Rover Defender L663 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the new-generation Defender L663 — from the four-cylinder diesel to the 400bhp six-cylinder petrol. Real UK costs, honest failure data, and the Ingenium tensioner risk that still haunts early units. Backed by 292+ real UK enquiries in 2025 [EM-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Land Rover Defender L663 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the new Defender — from the 2.0-litre four-cylinder Ingenium units to the 5.0-litre supercharged V8 and the new 4.4-litre twin-turbo V8.

overview.intro: The L663 is the second generation of the modern Defender, launched in 2019 as a radical departure from the traditional body-on-frame design. Built on Jaguar Land Rover's D7x aluminium monocoque architecture, it introduced independent suspension, advanced electronics, and a full range of Ingenium petrol and diesel engines — marking the first time a Defender offered V8 power since the original.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: JLR D7x aluminium monocoque • Predecessor: Land Rover Defender (L316) — original "old" Defender • Successor: Current model — no successor announced • Sibling body styles in this generation: Defender 90 (3-door), Defender 110 (5-door), Defender 130 (extended 5-door)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The L663 Defender generated 292 real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [EM-VERIFIED], concentrated on the 3.0-litre diesel six-cylinder as these vehicles exit manufacturer warranty.

items.quote: The six-cylinder Ingenium diesel is genuinely more refined and responsive than the four-cylinder, with early reliability surveys suggesting it is more dependable than previous Land Rover diesel engines
items.whoItsFor: Buyers who need towing capability without the four-cylinder's risk profile.

items.quote: The mild-hybrid straight-six delivers 400bhp with smooth, linear power and the 48-volt electric supercharger virtually eliminates lag
items.whoItsFor: Buyers who want effortless performance and the most polished driving experience in the range.

items.quote: The four-cylinder Ingenium diesel has a documented reputation for timing chain tensioner failure, with cold-start rattle as the classic early warning
items.whoItsFor: Only buyers with full service history and documented chain work.

items.quote: The supercharged AJ133 V8 is a complex, high-performance unit. When it fails — typically timing chain tensioner wear on pre-2013 design or supercharger coupler wear — repairs require specialist intervention and deep pockets
items.whoItsFor: Buyers who can absorb five-figure repair bills.

items.quote: The entry-level six-cylinder diesel offers the refinement of the D300 with slightly lower output, making it the sweet spot for most UK buyers who want six-cylinder torque without the highest purchase price.
items.whoItsFor: Buyers who prioritise refinement and reliability over outright power.

items.quote: The four-cylinder petrol Ingenium is often dismissed in favour of the six-cylinder P400, but it delivers 300bhp from a 2.0-litre and avoids the diesel emissions complexity entirely
items.whoItsFor: Buyers who want petrol reliability without the V8's running costs.

ownershipEconomics.h2: Land Rover Defender L663 — Ownership Economics

rows.ownershipVerdict: Risk vs Reward — chain history essential
Best Diesel — the L663's most dependable engine option
Solid Choice — simpler than diesels, fewer emissions components
Most Expensive — outstanding performance, outstanding running costs

economicsRule.title: The L663 rule
economicsRule.text: A £4,500–£7,500 D300 recon against a £40,000 L663 sits at 11–19% of the car's value — well within our 60% Land Rover threshold. Against a £70,000 example, the same recon drops to 6–11% — a routine maintenance decision, not a financial one. Value the car first, but on an L663, replacement almost always makes sense.

keyTakeaways.question: Which L663 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: 2.0 P300 petrol — no DPF, no AdBlue, simpler emissions system.

keyTakeaways.question: Which L663 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: 508PS 5.0 V8 — supercharger and timing chain repairs are specialist-only.

keyTakeaways.question: Which L663 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: 3.0 D250 — six-cylinder refinement without the D300's purchase premium.

problems.title: 204DT 2.0 Ingenium Diesel Timing Chain Tensioner
problems.affectedModels: L663 Defender D200, D240 (2019–present, pre-2020 units highest risk).
problems.rootCause: The 2.0-litre Ingenium diesel's timing chain tensioner can fail prematurely, allowing chain slack that leads to rattling on cold start. If ignored, the chain can skip teeth or snap, causing catastrophic piston-to-valve contact.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of cold-start rattle on a 2.0-litre diesel L663, get the timing system inspected immediately. An £800–£1,800 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £3,500–£5,800 replacement that follows if ignored. On an L663, the vehicle value easily justifies the repair.

cta.label: Compare 204DT engine prices for your Defender L663 →

problems.title: AJ20D6 3.0 Diesel Turbocharger & Emissions System
problems.rootCause: The twin-turbo setup on the 3.0-litre diesel places considerable stress on both turbo units, with some owners reporting turbo failure as early as 33,000 miles. The complex emissions system — including DPF, EGR, and AdBlue — is also prone to clogging on vehicles used primarily for short journeys.
problems.recommendation: If your 3.0-litre diesel L663 is used primarily for short urban journeys, budget for regular DPF regeneration and consider an extended motorway run every few weeks. Watch for whistling noises under acceleration or reduced power — these are early turbo warning signs.

cta.label: Compare AJ20D6 engine prices for your Defender L663 →

problems.title: PT306 3.0 P400 MHEV Belt & Cooling System
problems.rootCause: The Belt-Integrated Starter Generator (BISG) and accessory drive belt can fail, with JLR investigating reports of belt shredding and tensioner failure. The cooling fan may also fail intermittently, potentially causing engine overheating.
problems.recommendation: Have the BISG belt and cooling fan checked at every major service. Listen for unusual noises from the accessory drive and monitor the temperature gauge — if the fan fails, the engine can overheat quickly.

cta.label: Compare PT306 engine prices for your Defender L663 →
